data.title: France's Mistral looking to raise €3B at €20B valuation
data.description:
French artificial intelligence startup Mistral AI is in talks to secure around €3B ($3.5B) in funding, valuing the company at roughly €20B, Bloomberg reported.

Discussions are still at a nascent stage, and the valuation could go higher depending on investor demand, the report said, citing people familiar with the matter.

The Paris-based company, which is widely considered Europe’s response to OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO), was valued at €11.7B when it raised money in September. The potential cash injection is expected to help the firm compete against Silicon Valley’s AI dominance.

Last month, Mistral CEO Arthur Mensch said that the company is considering designing its own chips and may eventually develop them. In an interview with CNBC, Mensch said that custom chips can significantly reduce the cost of deploying tokens. The firm currently relies on Nvidia (NVDA) for computing power.

Mistral has already started building its own data centers in Europe, with locations in France and Sweden. It is aiming to generate $1.16B in revenue in 2026.

data.title: Real estate reaches all-time high; here are the companies fueling the rise
data.description:
Real estate (XLRE) has surpassed its 2021 all-time high of $44.85, as recent data from Freddie Mac showed that mortgages rose from last week.

The sector is currently $45.43—after reaching a record of $45.45—up 12.6% year-to-date and up 8.2% from a year ago.

 

 

 

Supporting the sector’s performance, home sales have risen even as mortgage rates increased this week. The 30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.52%, up from 6.48% last week, while the 15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.84%, up from 5.79% a week ago.

The median U.S. home sale prices reached a record $400,894 during the four weeks ending in June 7, up 1.5% year-over-year.

“Stronger employment momentum has helped existing home sales reach a five-month high,” said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac’s chief economist. “Importantly, we're seeing homebuyers look past the short-term rate fluctuations and actively enter the market, signaling renewed confidence in homeownership opportunities.”

Investors have rotated into defensives, including real estate, as some have sought to reduce technology exposure.

In addition, real estate has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) on a year-to-date basis. Numbers… with data centers and some of the sector’s top players—including Welltower (WELL), Equinix (EQIX), Digital Realty (DLR), and Prologis (PLD)—supporting its performance.

Below are the top-performing real estate stocks with a Seeking Alpha Quant rating of “buy” or “strong buy.”

  1. EPR Properties (EPR) +2.77%, Quant rating: 4.60
  2. Newmark Group (NMRK) +2.36%, Quant rating: 3.81
  3. Postal Realty Trust (PSTL) +2.03%, Quant rating: 4.86
  4. Chatham Lodging Trust (CLDT) +1.74%, Quant rating: 4.73
  5. Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) +1.66%, Quant rating: 4.81
  6. W. P. Carey (WPC) +1.65%, Quant rating: 3.92
  7. LXP Industrial Trust (LXP) +1.56%, Quant rating: 4.68
  8. Brixmor Property Group (BRX) +1.43%, Quant rating: 3.50
  9. Healthcare Realty Trust (HR) +1.41%, Quant rating: 4.44
  10. Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE) +1.37%, Quant rating: 4.78

Real Estate ETFs: (VNQ), (XLRE), (IYR), (USRT), and (HOMZ).

data.title: AXT surges following report on possible InP constraints
data.description:
AXT (AXTI) surged 10% during early market action on Friday following a report that indium phosphide, or InP, supplies might face constraints due to China's reluctance to issue export licenses, which could dampen the growth of optics. 

AXT produces InP and other compounds that are critical in the production of wafers. The company benefits from a vertically integrated supply chain, partially owning raw material companies in China that provide essential inputs for its production process. This strategy ensures a reliable supply of critical materials and cost advantages.

"With regards to InP, we don't see constraints or concerns around the timing and volume of China export license grants as new news, but we do see the additional focus being placed on Chinese restrictions as interesting," said Wedbush analysts Matt Bryson and Antoine Legault in an investor note. "In our view, US pressure has seemingly moderated potential other material constraints (e.g., rare earths)."

"Increasing the importance of InP as part of the US trade agenda could ease supply tightness with positive implications for AXT (remember AXT has chosen in recent quarters to guide revenues in line with already granted licenses, creating significant upside should license approvals accelerate)," they added.

What's more, if InP prices do increase, which are not currently added into AXT's consensus figures, it could provide significant upside.

Wedbush rates AXT with an Outperform rating and a $93 price target.

data.title: Triple Flag buys $440M gold stream on Ravenswood mine, raises 2030 production outlook
data.description:
Triple Flag Precious Metals (TFPM) up 3.4% in Friday's trading after saying it agreed to acquire a gold stream on the Ravenswood mine in Australia for US$440M in cash.

Under the deal terms, Triple Flag (TFPM) is entitled to purchase 5.5% of payable gold from the Ravenswood mine under the stream, which steps down to 3.75% after 194.2K oz of gold has been delivered, and then to 2.5% after 253K oz of gold have been delivered; the company will make ongoing payments of 10% of the spot gold price for each ounce delivered until 194.2K oz has been delivered, and 20% of the spot gold price thereafter.

Ravenswood is one of Australia's 10 largest gold mines by ore reserves, with current proved and probable ore reserves of 147M metric tons at 0.61 g/t containing 2.8M oz of gold.

The mine produced 134K oz of gold in 2025 and is expected to produce more than 200K oz/year by 2028 following a recent A$830M investment.

Following the addition of Ravenswood, Triple Flag (TFPM) increased its 2030 production outlook to 150K-160K gold equivalent oz from 140K-150K GEOs previously.

Ravenswood is jointly owned by operator EMR Capital and Golden Energy and Resources.

data.title: Williams-Sonoma sits at a demographic “sweet spot” — BofA Securities
data.description:
With its “affordable luxury” positioning, Williams-Sonoma (WSM) is in a “demographic sweet spot” and can capitalize on the resilience of its core customer with a household income of ~$150K, BofA Securities’ Christopher Nardone said, reinstating coverage of the stock with a Buy rating and $250 price target. 

With a portfolio that includes Pottery Barn, West Elm, Pottery Barn Kids & Teens, and the Williams-Sonoma banner, the company enjoys revenue diversification that doesn’t rely on strength in the housing market. However, any improvement in the housing market would unlock a potential upside to Nardone’s 10% EPS growth estimate for 2027 and 2028.

“We are most encouraged by the momentum at West Elm due to its category expansion, product refreshes, and successful collaborations; we think the momentum is sustainable,” Nardone writes.

And near-term, Nardone expects tariff pressures to ease in the second half with May freight rates already embedded in the company’s FY26 guidance. Williams-Sonoma (WSM) sees revenue increasing 2.7% to 6.7% to be within a range of $8.02B and $8.33B, and comparable sales growth of 2% to 6% versus Nardone’s estimate of 4% to 5% comparable sales growth over the next 2-3 years.

Shares are up ~1% on Friday, setting an all-time high of $222.50.

data.title: Rebuilding the tariff wall with Section 301
data.description:
The Supreme Court struck down President Trump's sweeping reciprocal tariffs earlier this year, but that has not deterred his administration from looking at alternatives to impose levies.

U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer proposed fresh tariffs on 60 economies following Section 301 investigations into forced labor and unfair trade practices.

The USTR proposed a 12.5% tariff rate on countries that allegedly failed to ban imports made with forced labor and a 10% tariff rate on six economies for failing to enforce such restrictions effectively. A 25% tariff on Brazil was also proposed, likely in addition to the 12.5% forced-labor levy.

The administration has not specified when these tariffs will take effect, but they are subject to public hearings, which will begin next month. To note, Trump's 10% global tariffs implemented under Section 122 will expire on July 24. 

The Atlantic Council estimated that a Section 301-based regime could generate up to $169B, assuming 2025 import levels. "This would broadly match, and even exceed, the estimated $166B collected under IEEPA in 2025," Madeline Chalecki, assistant director at the think tank's GeoEconomics Center, noted. 

"Our scenario, however, depends on a number of assumptions that may not hold. Ongoing negotiations could yield exemptions, trading partners could retaliate or secure carveouts, and courts could narrow executive authority," she added. 

The International Labor Organization's most recent estimates showed that 27.6M people were in forced labor, with Asia and the Pacific region accounting for 15.1M. 

But the U.S. has also faced challenges in enforcing its own forced labor laws. The U.S. accounted for a "disproportionately large share" of imports of goods at risk of being made with forced labor, according to a 2025 report by the Homeland Security Operational Analysis Center.

"In 2021, for example, the U.S. accounted for about 23% of those imports, by value, but it accounted for only about 13% of all global imports," the report noted. 

A separate Section 301 investigation into structural excess capacity is ongoing, which is looking into whether 16 economies – including China and the EU – are overproducing goods and hurting U.S. manufacturing.

But the question remains – will the Section 301 tariffs withstand legal challenges? Alan Wolff, former deputy director-general of the World Trade Organization, doesn't think so.

"A vulnerability of the use of Section 301 to restore the Trump 'reciprocal' tariffs is that it will be clear to the courts that this is mainly an attempt once again to transfer the full tariff power from the Congress to the president," Wolff wrote in a blog post for the Peterson Institute for International Economics. 

"The fact remains: Section 301 cannot be deployed to reach a result that it does not authorize – exercise by the executive branch of the country's full tariff power," he added.

data.title: Trump accuses Iran of 'dishonorable' conduct over leaked deal terms
data.description:
Just one day after signaling that an agreement with Iran was nearly complete, U.S. President Donald Trump on Friday lashed out at Tehran, accusing Iranian officials of misrepresenting the terms of the proposed deal.

"The terms that Iran leaked out to the Fake News have NOTHING to do with the terms that were agreed to, in writing," Trump wrote on social media. "What they said, including their weak and pathetic statement on having a deal, bears no relation to the truth. Very dishonorable people to deal with. With them, there is no such thing as dealing in good faith."

The president's sharp rebuke came after Iranian state media outlets published details about the framework of the agreement earlier Friday. According to those reports, Iran would not commit to ceding management of the Strait of Hormuz, and the deal would require the release of $24B in frozen Iranian funds.

Trump specifically addressed the Hormuz issue, calling Iran's continued targeting of ships in the strategic waterway "TOTALLY UNACCEPTABLE." He warned that "they better get their act together, and FAST!"

Energy prices were in the red Friday, but pared gains from earlier in the session, amid optimism that tensions could ease and shipping through the Strait of Hormuz could resume more normally. Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were down -0.7% to $87 a barrel at press time, while Brent Futures (CO1:COM) were down -0.9% to $89.6/barrel.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Sam Bankman-Fried loses appeal of fraud conviction
data.description:
Sam Bankman-Fried, who co-founded crypto exchange FTX, failed to convince a three-judge panel that his conviction of fraud should be overturned, according to the federal appeals court ruling on Friday.

The co-founder of the exchange that collapsed in late 2022 argued that he didn't receive a fair trial due to improper evidentiary rulings, Bloomberg News reported. Bankman-Fried was sentenced to 25 years in prison after a jury  found him guilty of defrauding customers and investors while he was running FTX, at the time the second-largest crypto exchange.

The one-time crypto mogul, who had promoted his image as an altruist and generous political donor, can still appeal to the full appeals court or to the U.S. Supreme Court.

During the appeal hearing in November, Bankman-Fried's attorney said the defendant wasn't allowed to testify about the role that attorneys played in preparing some documents. At the time, the judges sounded skeptical that that would have changed the verdict.

Earlier this week, Fox Business reported that Bankman-Fried also applied to the White House for a pardon.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: Space stocks, ADBE, and more
data.description:
Stock futures were mixed Friday as investors tracked easing geopolitical tensions and a pullback in safe-haven demand while also bracing for SpaceX’s IPO. Markets welcomed signs of progress in U.S.-Iran talks, which eased immediate concerns about disruption in the Strait of Hormuz.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • First Advantage (FA) +5% - Shares climbed after S&P Dow Jones Indices announced the company will replace Kennedy-Wilson Holdings ((KW)) in the S&P SmallCap 600 effective before the market opens on June 16. The change follows the pending acquisition of Kennedy-Wilson by a consortium led by its CEO and Fairfax Financial Holdings, which is expected to close soon. Shares of First Advantage gained as inclusion in the index is likely to drive incremental demand from index-tracking funds and ETFs.

  • AMD (AMD) +3% - Shares rose after Citi upgraded the semiconductor company to Buy from Neutral and raised its price target to $575 from $460, citing growing confidence in AMD's position in both AI GPUs and data center CPUs. Analyst Atif Malik said AMD is increasingly emerging as a credible second source in the AI GPU market and could capture a significant share of Meta's AI infrastructure spending, while also benefiting from the expanding market for agentic AI-driven CPUs. Citi now sees AMD as a major beneficiary of a long-term CPU renaissance due to its performance leadership, higher core counts, broad product portfolio, and x86 ecosystem strength, with investors potentially underappreciating the company's GPU opportunity heading into 2028.

Biggest stock losers

  • Virgin Galactic (SPCE) -23% and Rocket Lab (RKLB) -7% - Space stocks reversed sharply as the SpaceX (SPCX) IPO hype gave way to rotation and reality, with Redwire (RDW), AST SpaceMobile (ASTS), and other commercial space names sliding in broad-based selling. The pullback reflects investor concern that capital is migrating directly into SpaceX rather than lifting the broader sector, raising questions about whether the IPO tailwind has now become a headwind for publicly traded space peers.
  • Adobe (ADBE) -9% - Shares dipped despite delivering a strong FQ2 beat and raising full-year guidance, as investors appeared to be looking for even stronger signs of AI monetization. The company reported adjusted EPS of $5.96 on $6.62B in revenue, topping expectations on both metrics, while annualized recurring revenue reached $27.1B. Adobe also issued FQ3 guidance and raised its FY2026 outlook above Wall Street estimates, projecting revenue of $26.5B-$26.6B and adjusted EPS of $24.35-$24.45.
  • Lennar (LEN) -3% - Shares fell after the homebuilder lowered its FY2026 home delivery forecast to 82,000-83,000 homes from approximately 85,000, citing persistent headwinds from elevated mortgage rates, affordability challenges, cautious consumer sentiment, and geopolitical uncertainty. While second-quarter results were mixed, investors focused on the softer outlook, with Lennar forecasting Q3 new orders of 21,000-22,000 homes and deliveries of 20,500-21,500 homes, both below consensus expectations. Management noted that higher energy prices and renewed inflation pressures continue to weigh on housing demand, reinforcing concerns about the pace of market recovery.

data.title: U.S. equities are mixed as traders focus on the SpaceX IPO
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages are mixed on Friday as traders gear up for the highly anticipated SpaceX (SPCX) initial public offering.

The blue chip Dow (DJI) was +0.2% the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was near even, and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.3%. 

From a sector-by-sector perspective, six of the 11 S&P segments were higher, with energy leading the charge. At the other end of the spectrum, consumer discretionary has struggled the most so far in the session. 

SpaceX priced its IPO at $135 per share, raising roughly $75 billion in what is set to become the largest IPO ever, with a valuation of about $1.77 trillion. The company is also reportedly allocating a smaller-than-expected portion of its blockbuster initial public offering to retail investors. 

Treasury yields moved higher. The U.S. 2-Year Treasury yield (US2Y) added 4 basis points to 4.10%. At the same time, the U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield (US10Y) climbed by 4 basis points to 4.49%, and the U.S. 30-Year Treasury yield (US30Y) advanced 3 basis points to 4.99%.

As for stocks that were on the move, shares of Arm Holdings (ARM) advanced by 5.5%, while shares of Adobe (ADBE) fell 8.7%.
